chunk_summary: Post-discharge nutrition for preterm and low birth weight infants focuses on tailored feeding strategies, supplementation, and growth monitoring to prevent growth faltering and deficiencies.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional needs of preterm and low birth weight infants post-discharge.
Learn appropriate supplementation protocols for vitamin D, iron, calcium, and phosphorus.
Identify strategies for monitoring growth and addressing growth faltering in preterm infants.
key_facts: Vitamin D supplementation: 400 IU/day for LBW (1500–2499 g) and 800 IU/day for VLBW (<1500 g) until 6 months.
Iron supplementation: 2–4 mg/kg/day from 2 weeks in VLBW infants and 2–3 mg/kg/day from 6–8 weeks in other LBW infants on human milk, continued to 6 months.
Use preterm/post-discharge formula (22–24 kcal/oz) or fortified breastmilk for inadequate weight gain.
Monitor growth using corrected age and preterm growth charts like the Fenton chart, transitioning to WHO standards later.
Review feeding and weight within 48–72 hours post-discharge, then weekly until stable growth trajectory is achieved.
reference_quote: First choice milk is mother’s own milk; if unavailable, use donor milk or a preterm/post-discharge formula strategy as advised by the discharging unit. (Source: ESPGHAN position paper)
document_key_topics: Post-discharge feeding strategies for preterm infants
